items.passage: Microorganisms are tiny living things that exist almost everywhere on Earth, from soil to the human body. Most bacteria are far too small to see without a powerful microscope. Many of these microbes help people by breaking down food inside the digestive system. Some species produce useful substances that doctors turn into modern medicines. Although a few types cause disease, the majority remain harmless or even beneficial to living organisms.

The ozone layer is a thin band of gas high in the atmosphere that shields the planet from harmful rays. It absorbs most of the ultraviolet light that comes from the Sun. Without this protection, that radiation would damage plants, animals, and human skin. During the 1980s, scientists discovered that certain chemicals were thinning the layer badly. Since then, global agreements have reduced those pollutants, and the layer is slowly beginning to recover.

The placebo effect occurs when a harmless treatment improves a patient simply because they expect it to work. In many trials, people given fake pills report feeling genuinely better. This response reveals how the mind can shape the body's physical state. Doctors must account for this powerful reaction when they test whether new drugs truly help. Understanding why belief alone can ease symptoms remains an important goal for medical researchers today.

The Silk Road was a vast network of trade routes that once linked China with distant lands to the west. Merchants carried silk, spices, and precious metals across deserts and towering mountain ranges. Along the way, they also spread new ideas, religions, and technologies between very different cultures. Busy market towns grew wealthy from the constant stream of foreign travelers and goods. Though the routes faded centuries ago, their lasting influence on world history is still felt.

Minerals are naturally formed solids that make up the rocks of the Earth's crust. Each mineral has a specific chemical makeup and an orderly internal arrangement of atoms. Geologists identify them by testing properties such as color, hardness, and the way light reflects. Some minerals form slowly as hot molten rock cools deep beneath the surface. Others develop when mineral-rich water evaporates and leaves solid crystals behind in cracks.

Cosmic rays are high-energy particles that travel through space and constantly strike the Earth's atmosphere. Most of them come from distant stars that exploded violently long ago. When these particles hit the air, they break apart into showers of smaller pieces. Special detectors on the ground and on satellites allow scientists to study this invisible rain. By tracking cosmic rays, researchers learn more about powerful events happening far across the universe.
